summary: Written as a fictional dialogue set in 2045 between a father and son discussing the COVID-19 pandemic, Martin argues that programmers were unsung heroes of the crisis. While acknowledging the frontline bravery of healthcare workers, grocery employees, and delivery drivers, he makes the case that software infrastructure—from internet communications to contactless payments to RNA sequencing—was essential to society's ability to shelter in place and eventually defeat the virus. The piece frames programming as a profession of quiet but profound societal importance.
key_points: Healthcare workers, grocery staff, and delivery drivers showed physical bravery by working despite infection risks
Programmers enabled remote work, communication, shopping, and entertainment that made sheltering at home viable
Software systems for contactless payment reduced virus transmission compared to paper money
RNA sequencing software gave researchers a head start on vaccine development
The pandemic was the first national emergency where instant digital communication reached almost everyone
money_quotes: They were as brave as any soldier going to war. Perhaps braver, because you couldn't see the enemy, and in those days you couldn't fight it.
Many of us did. We were the lucky ones.
Even though most people weren't working, the essential parts of our civilization had to be kept running.
It was the programmers who made it possible for people to work from home; because it was the programmers who built the software that made the internet possible.
This was the first full scale national emergency during which people had instant access to the news, to the government, and to each other.
Without the programmers who made those systems, people would have had a much harder time sheltering at home; and the pandemic would have been much worse.
It's an important job, Son. I never want to be anything else. Except, of course, your Dad.
key_insight: While programmers weren't frontline heroes risking their lives, the digital infrastructure they built was essential to society's ability to survive the pandemic by enabling people to work, communicate, shop, and be entertained from the safety of their homes.
